era_context: The Yuan Dynasty in China continues to decline as Ming forces gain strength. The Hundred Years' War between England and France remains active with shifting military fortunes. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia. In Europe, the Papacy is undergoing institutional changes. The Castilian Civil War is ongoing. This is a period of political and military transition across multiple regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian steppes (outside major trade routes)
Southeast Asia (sparse documentation in European sources)

events.title: Castilian Civil War: Henry of Trastámara challenges Peter of Castile
events.description: The Castilian Civil War intensifies as Henry of Trastámara, an illegitimate son of Alfonso XI, challenges the legitimacy of Peter of Castile and seeks to seize the throne. The conflict draws in external powers, including England (supporting Peter) and France (supporting Henry). The civil war represents a significant internal conflict within the Iberian Peninsula and has implications for the broader Hundred Years' War, as both England and France use the conflict to advance their strategic interests.
key_figures: Henry of Trastámara (claimant to Castilian throne)
Edward, the Black Prince (English supporter of Peter)
events.certainty_note: The Castilian Civil War and Henry of Trastámara's challenge to Peter are well-documented in contemporary sources. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty continues to lose territory to Ming forces
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty faces continued military losses to Ming forces under Zhu Yuanzhang. Ming armies conduct campaigns in central and northern China, systematically reducing Yuan-controlled territory. The Yuan Dynasty's military capacity is weakened by internal instability, economic decline, and the defection of regional commanders to the Ming cause. This period marks the accelerating decline of Yuan power and the consolidation of Ming control over the Chinese heartland.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Yuan Shih (History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general military situation in 1366 is well-attested, though specific campaign details may vary between sources. The date is approximate within the broader context of Ming military expansion.

events.title: Urban V becomes Pope; continuation of Avignon Papacy
events.description: Guillaume de Grimoard is elected Pope and takes the name Urban V. Urban V continues the Avignon Papacy, maintaining the papal residence in Avignon rather than Rome. His papacy is marked by efforts to reform Church administration and address corruption, though his health problems limit his effectiveness. Urban V's papacy represents a continuation of the institutional changes that have characterized the Avignon period.
events.certainty_note: Urban V's election in 1362 is well-documented, but the date given here (1366) appears to be incorrect. Modern scholarship places his election in 1362. This entry may represent a dating error.

events.title: Ottoman forces consolidate control in Anatolia; expansion toward Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I continue military campaigns to consolidate control in Anatolia and begin expansion into the Balkans. The campaigns involve the subjugation of rival Turkish beyliks and the expansion of Ottoman territory at the expense of declining Byzantine and Serbian holdings. This period marks the Ottoman Empire's emergence as a significant military and political force in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, setting the stage for later Ottoman dominance.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman military expansion in this period is attested in Ottoman and Byzantine sources, though specific campaign details and dates can vary by ±1–2 years.

events.title: Petrarch's literary influence spreads throughout Italy; humanist movement gains momentum
events.description: Francesco Petrarch's literary works and humanist philosophy continue to gain influence throughout Italy, inspiring a new generation of scholars and patrons. Petrarch's emphasis on classical learning, individual achievement, and the recovery of ancient texts becomes increasingly influential in Italian intellectual circles. This period marks the consolidation of humanist scholarship as a major intellectual movement in Italy, laying the groundwork for the broader Renaissance.

sources.name: Contemporary biographical accounts and letters from Petrarch's correspondents

events.certainty_note: Petrarch's literary influence in 1366 is well-documented through his surviving letters and the accounts of his contemporaries. The date is firmly established.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Urban V's election is given as 1366 in this entry, but modern scholarship places it in 1362. This represents a significant dating error. The significance of Ottoman expansion in 1366 is sometimes overstated; the Ottoman Empire's major expansion into the Balkans occurs primarily after 1389. The Castilian Civil War is sometimes portrayed as a simple conflict between two claimants, but it was more complex, involving multiple factions and regional powers.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1366 is uneven. European sources (particularly English, Castilian, and Italian) are relatively rich, while sources for Asia and Africa are sparser. Chinese sources for the Yuan Dynasty's decline are abundant but reflect the perspective of the victorious Ming Dynasty. Ottoman sources for this period are limited and often compiled centuries later. The dating of papal elections in this entry appears to be incorrect, suggesting that medieval papal chronology requires careful verification. Sub-Saharan African and Pacific history remain largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Castilian Civil War leads to the Battle of Nájera in 1367
Yuan military losses culminate in the Ming Dynasty founding in 1368
Urban V's papacy is followed by Gregory XI's election in 1367
Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans sets the stage for the Battle of Kosovo
